q: What does the longer line in the symbol of an electric cell represent?
a: The positive terminal of the cell.

q: What does the shorter, thicker line in the symbol of an electric cell represent?
a: The negative terminal of the cell.

q: What is a battery?
a: A combination of two or more electric cells connected together, with the positive terminal of one cell connected to the negative terminal of the next.

q: What is a circuit diagram?
a: A representation of an electric circuit using standard symbols for its components.

q: When is a circuit said to be closed?
a: When the switch is in the ON position and the circuit from positive to negative terminal is complete, allowing current to flow.

q: When is a circuit said to be open?
a: When the switch is in the OFF position, making the circuit incomplete and stopping all current flow.

q: What is the filament of an electric bulb?
a: A thin wire inside the bulb that glows when electric current passes through it. When it breaks, the bulb is fused.

q: What is the heating effect of electric current?
a: When electric current flows through a wire, the wire gets heated. This is called the heating effect of electric current.

q: What factors affect the amount of heat produced in a wire?
a: The material, length, and thickness of the wire.

q: What is the element in an electric appliance?
a: The coil of wire inside appliances like heaters, irons, and geysers that becomes red hot and gives out heat when current flows.

q: Name four appliances that use the heating effect of electric current.
a: Electric iron, immersion heater, electric kettle, geyser (also: hotplate, room heater, hair dryer).

q: Which type of light bulb is the most electricity efficient?
a: LED (Light Emitting Diode) bulbs consume the least electricity and are the most efficient.

q: Why are incandescent bulbs less efficient than LEDs?
a: Incandescent bulbs produce both light and unwanted heat, wasting a part of the electricity consumed.

q: What is a short circuit?
a: When the insulation on wires wears off and bare wires touch each other, allowing excessive current to flow — this can cause fires.

q: What is an overload in an electrical circuit?
a: When too many devices are connected to a single socket, causing excessive current flow that can overheat wires and cause fires.

q: What is an electric fuse?
a: A safety device made of a special wire that melts and breaks the circuit when current exceeds the safe limit, preventing damage and fires.

q: What are MCBs and how do they work?
a: Miniature Circuit Breakers are switches that automatically turn off when current exceeds the safe limit. They can be turned back on, unlike fuses which must be replaced.

q: What is the magnetic effect of electric current?
a: When electric current flows through a wire, the wire behaves like a magnet. This is the magnetic effect of electric current.

q: Who first discovered the magnetic effect of electric current?
a: Hans Christian Oersted (1777–1851), who noticed that a compass needle deflects whenever current is passed through a nearby wire.

q: What is an electromagnet?
a: A current-carrying coil of insulated wire wrapped around a piece of iron. It behaves like a magnet when current flows and loses magnetism when current is switched off.

q: Name three uses of electromagnets.
a: In cranes for lifting heavy magnetic loads; in separating magnetic material from junk; by doctors to remove magnetic particles from eyes (also in toys and electric bells).

q: How does an electric bell work?
a: When current flows, the electromagnet attracts an iron strip whose hammer strikes the gong. This breaks the circuit, the electromagnet loses magnetism, the strip returns, and the cycle repeats rapidly.

q: What is the ISI mark?
a: A Standard Mark assigned by the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) on products, assuring they meet the required quality specifications.

q: Why should fluorescent tubes and CFLs be disposed of carefully?
a: Because they contain mercury vapour, which is toxic in nature.

q: What happens to the compass needle when a switch in a nearby circuit is turned ON?
a: The compass needle deflects from its north-south position because the current-carrying wire behaves like a magnet.

q: Why can't an ordinary wire be used as a fuse?
a: A fuse wire is specially designed to melt at the correct current limit. An ordinary wire may not melt in time, failing to protect the circuit from overload or short circuit.

q: What does the symbol for a switch in the ON position look like compared to the OFF position?
a: In the ON position, the switch symbol shows a closed/connected gap. In the OFF position, the gap is open/broken, representing an incomplete circuit.

q: Who is credited with inventing the electric bulb?
a: Thomas Alva Edison (1847–1931), who also invented the gramophone, motion picture camera, and carbon transmitter.

q: What is the function of a fuse in an electrical circuit?
a: A fuse protects electrical circuits by melting and breaking the circuit when current exceeds the safe limit, preventing fire and damage to appliances.

q: Why does the electromagnet in an electric bell lose its magnetism repeatedly?
a: When the electromagnet pulls the iron strip, it breaks the circuit, stopping current flow. Without current, the coil is no longer an electromagnet and loses its magnetism.
